_comment: Key illustrated events for Chapter 16 — The Cleverest Letter. Verified spine: 1649 Shivaji wrote to Mughal prince Murad Bakhsh (son of Shah Jahan) professing loyalty, making Bijapur fear the Bhonsles would defect to the Mughals; Bijapur freed Shahaji (May 1649); price = Shivaji returned Kondhana + promised to sit quietly. First contact with the Mughal empire (his future great enemy). Letter wording not extant; dramatised. Antagonists/empires shown as dignified individuals, never a people/faith. Shivaji = the LOCKED adult young-man ref (full beard, sturdy, pearls, established jiretop, chandrakor). 'anchor' is an EXACT substring of chapters/ch16.md. 'refs' are entity slugs from reference/index.json.

events.title: Three giants, not one
events.scene_prompt: Indoors at the Lal Mahal by lamplight: Shivaji (the locked adult young-man — sturdy build, full thick black beard and bold moustache, iconic cream/white jiretop turban with rear peak + tassel, pearl necklaces, chandrakor on forehead) leans intently over a large hand-drawn map of the whole Deccan / India spread on a low table, studying it, deep in calculation — realising the board has THREE great powers, not one. Thoughtful, strategic, a plan forming. Warm lamplight, papers and a reed pen. Matches his young-man reference exactly.

events.title: The cleverest letter
events.anchor: pressed his own seal into it
events.scene_prompt: A pivotal, charged moment by warm lamplight: Shivaji (locked adult young-man ref — sturdy, full black beard and bold moustache, iconic cream/white jiretop turban with rear peak + tassel, pearl necklaces, chandrakor) has just finished writing the fateful letter and presses his octagonal RAJMUDRA royal seal into a blob of warm red wax on the folded cream letter, the fresh octagonal seal impression with its Devanagari script visible (matching the Rajmudra reference). A faint, knowing, sober half-smile — he understands the size of what he is doing. The big honest Tanaji Malusare stands beside him, watching, slowly realising the cleverness of the plan. Intimate, momentous, clever-but-grave mood. Warm lamp glow. Matches the references exactly.

events.title: The empire takes notice
events.anchor: the boy in the hills was now a name the Mughal court knew
events.scene_prompt: In a grand, opulent Mughal audience hall (matching the Mughal-court reference — white marble and red sandstone, cusped arches, refined imperial grandeur, distinct from the Deccan Bijapur court): the young Mughal prince Murad Bakhsh (matching his reference — fine Mughal court robes, jewelled feathered turban, pearls, faint halo) sits/stands reading Shivaji's folded letter, an attendant nearby, his expression coolly intrigued. The sense of a vast, mighty empire turning its attention, for the first time, toward a small clever player far to the south. Magnificent, cool, powerful mood. Depict the prince and courtiers with full dignity, as individuals. Vary the courtiers' faces.

events.title: Freed by a folded paper
events.anchor: walked out of his imprisonment a free man
events.scene_prompt: Shahaji Bhonsle (matching his reference — full black beard, tall plumed Deccan turban, dignified) walking FREE, his chains struck off, leaving his imprisonment at last — upright, dignified, relieved, his weapons and honour restored, perhaps a Bijapur official gesturing him out. A moment of release and quiet vindication — freed not by an army but by his son's cleverness. Hopeful, grave-but-warm mood; grand stone interior / gateway, cool light brightening. No violence, no gore. Vary background faces; individuals, not caricatures.

events.title: The price, paid in stone
events.anchor: he handed the mountain back to Bijapur
events.scene_prompt: Bittersweet daytime scene at the great hill-fort of Kondhana (matching the Kondhana reference): the saffron/orange Maratha banner being lowered/handed down from the fort as it is given back to Bijapur — the price of Shahaji's freedom. Shivaji (locked adult young-man ref — sturdy, full beard, iconic jiretop, pearls, chandrakor) stands looking up at the fort he is surrendering, his expression composed but privately pained and resolute (a silent promise to return). A few Maval men and Bijapuri officers present, dignified, no conflict. Muted, wistful, dignified mood; natural daylight, the Sahyadri ranges beyond. Matches the references exactly.
